name: Vale Generational References
summary: Vale's generational references: every allocation has a 48-bit generation counter; pointers carry the expected generation. Dereferencing checks expected vs current; mismatch traps. ~2–10% runtime cost without GC, RC, or borrow checker. Region borrowing eliminates checks within blocks proven safe.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Evan Ovadia's **Vale** uses **generational references** as its primary memory-safety mechanism. Every allocation has a 48-bit generation counter; references carry the generation expected at allocation. Dereferencing a generational reference checks the expected generation against the current one; if they don't match, the reference points at memory that has been reused for something else, and the access traps.

The approach trades runtime overhead (typically 2–10% on real workloads) for the simplicity of having no GC, no RC, and no borrow checker. Allocations can be freed eagerly at end-of-scope without tracking references; existing references just become invalid (and trap on next use).

sections.title: Region borrowing prototype
sections.content:
The first region-borrowing prototype shipped July 2023: pure functions and `region` blocks can temporarily make data immutable, eliminating generation checks for code inside the block. The August 2025 **Group Borrowing** proposal (Nick Smith, endorsed by Ovadia) is a *zero-overhead* alternative to aliasable-xor-mutable — adds mutable aliasing without RC/GC/generational refs, described as cleaner than Vale's regions, still draft.

Hybrid-Generational Memory was effectively abandoned after ~32 iterations; Ovadia explicitly says regions + generational refs subsumed it.

Annotation burden is opt-in: programs run without regions; add `pure`/region annotations only where you want zero generation checks. This is the "regions as immutability projection" design point — distinct from regions as deallocation discipline.
